system.biography:
Physical Description

Photios stands five feet seven inches with a scholar's build—slight of frame but not frail, carrying himself with the careful posture of someone who spends long hours bent over manuscripts and maps. At thirty-six, premature grey already streaks his black hair, a physical manifestation of the melancholy that has marked him since childhood. His brown eyes are intelligent and observant, constantly assessing—the "good eye" his colleagues recognize, noticing details others miss while simultaneously cataloging everything that could go wrong.

His face is angular and aristocratic in the Byzantine manner, with high cheekbones and a neatly trimmed beard kept in the Orthodox scholarly fashion. His hands are a cartographer's tools—long-fingered, precise, perpetually ink-stained. He moves deliberately, economically, wasting no motion. When concentrating, he traces invisible patterns in the air or on surfaces, mapping thoughts as naturally as geography.

He dresses in the Byzantine style despite his current refugee status, clinging to the visual markers of his lost civilization. Dark, somber colors—deep blues, blacks, subtle purples—reflect both Jerbiton aesthetic sensibility and personal melancholy. His clothing is well-made but practical for travel, expensive fabrics in sensible cuts. He wears a small silver Orthodox cross on a chain, one of the few physical objects he saved from Constantinople.

His pupils turn bright gold when he casts spells, the color slowly fading like ink dissolving in water—a sigil both beautiful and unsettling, as if his eyes briefly become the illuminated capitals in a manuscript before returning to mundane brown.

Born: 1169, Anatolia

House: Jerbiton (no league affiliation after Constantinople's fall)

Former Covenant: Mouseion Prometheia, Constantinople (destroyed 1204)

Parens: Anna Komnene of Constantinople (granddaughter of the Anna Komnene)

Current Status (1205): Recently arrived in North Sea region with Solving of Criamon and Khalida al-Maghribiyya after fleeing Constantinople together.

Childhood in Anatolia (1169-1178)

Born as Theodoros in Anatolia to a family of modest means, he showed early aptitude for observation and pattern recognition. His Gift manifested around age six, and his family—recognizing both the danger and opportunity—sought someone who could train him properly.

Through scholarly networks, they learned of Anna Komnene, a respected Jerbiton maga in Constantinople who was accepting a new apprentice. The family made the difficult journey to the great city, where Anna recognized Theodoros's potential—not just magical talent, but the precise, meticulous mind of a natural scholar and the careful temperament suited to Jerbiton's diplomatic traditions.

She took him as apprentice in 1178, when he was nine years old, and gave him the name Photios ("luminous one") to mark his new life—a name he's kept, though it feels increasingly ironic given his melancholic nature.

Apprenticeship at Mouseion Prometheia (1178-1193)

The Covenant

Mouseion Prometheia was one of Constantinople's great achievements—a covenant dedicated to the preservation and transmission of knowledge, named after both the ancient Library of Alexandria and the Titan who gave fire to humanity. Located in Constantinople itself, it housed an extensive library of both Greek and Latin texts and served as a center for scholarly exchange between Byzantine and Western magical traditions.

The covenant maintained close ties with the secular libraries and scriptoria of Constantinople, serving as bridge between Hermetic magic and Byzantine scholarship. It specialized in Intellego magic, preservation of texts, and the kind of subtle diplomatic magic that Jerbiton excels at.

Anna Komnene

Photios's parens was the granddaughter of the famous Byzantine historian Anna Komnene. She embodied Jerbiton ideals—scholarly, cultured, politically astute, and committed to preserving civilization's achievements.

She saw in young Theodoros/Photios a kindred spirit—the same careful observation, the same love of knowledge, the same tendency toward melancholy contemplation. She trained him not just in magic but in the whole of Byzantine scholarly tradition: Greek philosophy, classical texts, cartography, theology, law.

Training and Development

Photios received a comprehensive Byzantine education:

From Anna: Hermetic theory with emphasis on Intellego and Imaginem, diplomatic magic suitable for Jerbiton, cartography both mundane and magical, and the art of preservation—how to make knowledge endure across time.

From Mouseion Prometheia's culture: Greek philosophical tradition (particularly Plato and Aristotle), Christian theology in the Orthodox mode, classical texts and their preservation, and the integration of Hermetic magic with Byzantine scholarly culture.

From Constantinople itself: He grew up in one of the world's great cities, surrounded by libraries, churches covered in mosaics, the imperial court, and the endless intellectual ferment of Byzantine culture. He learned to navigate complex political situations, to appreciate beauty, and to understand how fragile civilization is.

He developed deep expertise in cartography, discovering he could create maps that recorded not just geography but genius loci—the spirit and character of places. His Intellego specialization combined with artistic talent (Free Expression) allowed him to make maps that were both accurate and evocative.

Short-Ranged Magic and Cyclic Limitations

During apprenticeship, two significant limitations became apparent:

Short-Ranged Magic: Photios's spells work best at Touch range. Anything beyond this is significantly less effective (halved Casting Total). This forced him to develop compensatory strategies:

  • Focus on spells that work through physical contact (maps he touches, items he holds)
  • Laboratory magic where range matters less
  • Careful positioning in any magical conflict
  • Reliance on enchanted items for ranged effects

Anna, recognizing this limitation, emphasized laboratory work and item creation over combat casting. A Jerbiton magus rarely needs to hurl fire—subtle enchantments and careful preparation serve better.

Cyclic Magic: His magic strengthens in winter and summer (+3 bonus) but weakens during spring and autumn (-3 penalty). This created interesting patterns:

  • Winter and summer for intensive laboratory work
  • Spring and autumn for study, correspondence, and non-magical activities
  • Learning to plan around magical fluctuations
  • Understanding magic as cyclical like seasons, not constant like sunlight

Anna taught him to see this not as limitation but as rhythm—magic flowing with natural cycles, stronger and weaker like tides.

The Age Quickly Curse

Late in his apprenticeship—around age twenty or twenty-one—Photios was afflicted by a Lesser Malediction whose nature took time to recognize. He began aging faster than normal, showing more grey hair, more fatigue, subtle signs of premature aging, and suffering from severe nightmares. Initially attributed to stress or overwork, Anna eventually realized he was under a curse. The cause remains unknown.

The curse means his effective age advances twice as fast as chronological age. By 1205 (chronological age 36), he appears and feels thirty-seven—not dramatic yet, but the progression is inexorable. Without intervention, he'll age into decrepitude while still chronologically middle-aged.

Anna researched potential remedies but found none before Photios' gauntlet. Creating a Longevity Ritual will be more difficult for him (must work against his effective age), but it's not impossible. Still, the curse adds urgency to everything he does—time is literally running faster for him than for others.

Post-Gauntlet Years (1193-1204)

Integration into Mouseion Prometheia

Photios passed his Gauntlet in 1193 at age twenty-four, becoming a full magus of House Jerbiton. He remained at Mouseion Prometheia, as it was his home and he had no other covenant to join. He developed his research program focusing on:

  • Magical cartography and the recording of genius loci
  • Preservation of texts through Intellego and Imaginem magic
  • Integration of Byzantine scholarship with Hermetic theory

His laboratory work was meticulous but slowed by his Short-Ranged limitation—many standard procedures worked poorly for him, forcing creative adaptations. His Cyclic Magic meant he learned to plan work around seasons, maximizing output during winter and summer, using shoulder seasons for study and correspondence.

Meeting Solving (1203-1203)

In 1203, Solving visited Constantinople. They spent months together, deepening their friendship. Solving met Anna Komnene and other magi at Mouseion Prometheia. Later, he was introduced to Khalida al-Maghribiyya, who had recently arrived from Al-Andalus.

The three of them—Solving, Photios, Khalida—formed the core of a small intellectual circle. They bonded over Avicenna (Khalida's specialty), Greek philosophy (Photios's domain), and mystical theology (Solving's interest). They debated late into the night, mapped the city's magical auras, and explored Constantinople's libraries together.

The Growing Shadow (1203-1204)

By late 1203, political tensions in Constantinople were rising. The Fourth Crusade, ostensibly headed for Egypt, was increasingly entangled in Byzantine politics. Photios, pessimistic by nature, saw danger coming. He began quietly securing valuable texts, making backup copies, preparing contingencies.

His colleagues at Mouseion Prometheia were less concerned—Constantinople had weathered political crises before, the Great Palace was impregnable, surely the Crusaders wouldn't actually attack fellow Christians...

Photios wasn't so sure. His "good eye" for detail, his tendency to see potential disasters, his study of history—all suggested this could end badly. He began transferring his most important work to portable formats, creating travel-ready maps, setting aside resources for flight.

His warnings were dismissed as typical Photios pessimism. Even Anna, who knew him well, thought he was overreacting.

April 1204: The Sack of Constantinople

When Solving experienced a premonition of catastrophe in early April 1204 and insisted they flee immediately, Photios believed him instantly. Not because he trusted Criamon visions (he's skeptical of prophecy), but because it confirmed his own fears.

He, Solving, and Khalida fled Constantinople on April 11, 1204—the day before the Crusaders breached the walls.

What happened next was everything Photios had feared and worse. The Fourth Crusade sacked Constantinople from April 12-15, 1204. The city that had stood for over 900 years, that had preserved classical learning through the Dark Ages, that housed irreplaceable libraries and treasures—was systematically looted and burned.

Whether the three of them witnessed the destruction from a distance or heard reports later, the impact was devastating. For Photios specifically:

His home was destroyed. Mouseion Prometheia burned. The libraries he'd spent his life preserving were gone. Colleagues and friends were killed or scattered.

Anna Komnene's fate is unknown. She may have died in the destruction, fled to another covenant, or been captured. Photios doesn't know, and this uncertainty haunts him.

Validation of pessimism, but no satisfaction. He'd been right that disaster was coming, had prepared as best he could, had even escaped—but being right brought no comfort. He'd wanted to be wrong.

The fragility of preservation. Everything he believed about protecting knowledge—build strong walls, maintain institutions, copy carefully, create permanence—had failed. Constantinople, second Rome, repository of classical wisdom, impregnable city—had fallen in three days.

This was Photios's foundational trauma, the event that shapes everything after. His pessimism was validated in the worst possible way. His life's work vanished in fire. The Age Quickly curse means he has less time to rebuild than others. And he's a refugee with no home to return to.

Aftermath and Formation of Sjórseiðr (1204-1205)

The three survivors—Solving, Photios, Khalida—fled together. Their shared trauma forged deep bonds:

With Solving: The philosophical debates took on new urgency. Photios had believed in permanence and institutional protection. Constantinople proved him wrong. Solving's ideas about mobility, about knowledge that survives by never staying still, suddenly seemed less abstract and more necessary. They began developing the concept that would become Sjórseiðr—a covenant that can't be sacked because it's never in one place.

With Khalida: Both had experienced the violent destruction of great centers of learning (Constantinople for Photios, the pressures on Al-Qasṣāṣ for Khalida). Both were refugees, both were scholars mourning lost libraries, both were trying to figure out what preservation means when permanence fails. They bonded over Islamic and Greek philosophical traditions, over maps and weather, over shared grief.

The Founding Insight: Watching Constantinople burn, Photios and Solving had their crucial realization: What if we build an Alexandria that can't burn because it never stays still? What if knowledge is developed not by walls and institutions, but by movement and transmission?

Photios still struggles with this philosophically—he wants to believe in permanence, in building something that lasts. But he can't deny the evidence: Constantinople fell. Staying still led to destruction. Maybe Solving is right about mobility.

Personality and Motivations (1205)

Core Traits

Pessimist (+2): Constantly anticipates disaster. "Of course the storm will sink us." "Obviously the authorities will arrest us." "The magical creature will definitely attack." This isn't performative cynicism—he genuinely sees all the ways things can go wrong and feels compelled to point them out. Colleagues find it exhausting, but he's often proven right.

Good Eye (+2): Notices details others miss. Spots the inconsistency in the map, the unusual magical signature, the subtle political undercurrent. This combines with his pessimism in interesting ways—he sees both the danger signs AND the potential solutions, but tends to emphasize the former.

Courteous (+1): Despite melancholy and pessimism, maintains Byzantine standards of courtesy and proper behavior. This is partly Jerbiton training (diplomacy requires politeness), partly cultural inheritance (Byzantine court culture valued refinement), and partly personal conviction (civilization matters more when chaos threatens).

Depressed (Minor Flaw): Sees little point in acting, takes no pleasure in achievements. This predates Constantinople's fall—he's been melancholic since childhood—but the destruction deepened it significantly. He functions, he works, he contributes, but joy eludes him. The Age Quickly curse adds existential dread: even if he builds something, he won't live long enough to see it endure.

Proud (Minor Flaw): Believes himself intellectually superior to most people, and more perceptive than they are (his Good Eye confirms this). He's a Byzantine scholar, trained in Constantinople, parens was a Komnene—he's actually better educated than most magi he encounters, and he knows it. This can make him insufferable, especially when his pessimistic predictions prove correct.

The Paradox

When things are going well, Photios is anxious, pessimistic, muttering about impending doom, hands trembling slightly as he checks and rechecks contingency plans.

When disaster actually strikes—storms, hostile authorities, magical attacks—he becomes unnaturally calm and efficient. His hands steady, his voice becomes clear, his mind focuses sharply. This he planned for. The worst has come, and he has a response ready.

It's the waiting for disaster that torments him. The disaster itself is almost a relief, because at least the uncertainty is over.

Religious Practice and True Faith

Photios is devoutly Orthodox Christian, though his theology is sophisticated and somewhat heterodox:

  • Performs Orthodox prayers and observances faithfully
  • Believes deeply in divine providence, though questions its benevolence
  • Sees his magical Gift as divine endowment (Gentle Gift supporting this interpretation)
  • Integrates Greek philosophy with Christian theology in Byzantine tradition

The Age Quickly curse creates theological questions he wrestles with:

  • Is this divine punishment for unknown sin?
  • Is suffering the price of wisdom?
  • Does God care about individual human lives, or only cosmic patterns?
  • If time is running out faster for him, what is he meant to accomplish?

Unlike Khalida's Sufi universalism, Photios's faith is more questioning, more troubled. He prays faithfully but expects no answers. He trusts God exists but isn't sure God is trustworthy.

Magical Approach

Laboratory-focused: Short-Ranged Magic makes him ineffective in magical combat. He compensates by:

  • Extensive preparation and enchanted items
  • Careful research and spell optimization
  • Creating tools others can use when his own casting is weak
  • Working primarily in the lab where his limitations matter less

Intellego Specialist: With Affinity, Puissant, and natural aptitude, he's exceptional at information-gathering magic. He sees the world through Intellego—sensing auras, detecting magic, understanding the essential nature of places and things. His maps aren't just geographical; they capture magical topology, genius loci, the "character" of locations.

Method Caster: He's perfected precise, consistent techniques for formulaic magic. Gets +3 bonus when using his standard methods, but this requires perfect conditions (correct gestures, proper voicing, no alterations). This suits his personality—he likes predictability, careful procedure, knowing exactly what will happen.

Cyclic Planning: Winter and summer are his power seasons (+3 bonus). Spring and autumn are weak (-3 penalty). He structures his year around this:

  • Winter/summer: intensive laboratory work, spell invention, major projects
  • Spring/autumn: study, writing, correspondence, minimal spellcasting
  • Plans covenant activities around his magical cycles when possible

Current Motivations (1205)

Preservation through documentation: If institutions fail, at least create records. His magical cartography project aims to document everywhere they visit—not just geography, but the magical, cultural, and spiritual character of places. If Sjórseiðr succeeds, they'll have created an atlas of the magical world. If it fails, at least the maps might survive.

Proving permanence is possible: Despite everything, he still wants to believe knowledge can be preserved through careful work. The mobile covenant challenges this belief, but maybe it's a new kind of permanence—knowledge that endures through movement rather than despite it.

Finding/mourning Anna Komnene: His parens's fate is unknown. Is she dead? Fled? Imprisoned? Part of him wants to know; part of him fears the answer. This is an unresolved grief that haunts him.

Managing the Age Quickly curse: He needs a Longevity Ritual eventually, but creating one that works against his effective age will be challenging. He's not desperate yet (only appearing 37 at chronological 36), but the timeline is compressed. He has less time than others to accomplish his goals.

Navigating philosophical conflict with Solving: Photios wants to believe in permanence, roots, stability. Solving believes in mobility, flow, ataraxia through non-attachment. They're founding a covenant based on Solving's philosophy, and Photios is still wrestling with whether it's wisdom or just running away.

Relationship with Other Founders

With Solving: They debate constantly—Photios bringing Greek philosophical rigor and Byzantine scholarship, Solving bringing Epicurean mysticism and Criamon insights. Their disagreements are productive: Photios challenges Solving's vague mysticism, Solving challenges Photios's rigid assumptions. When disaster strikes, they complement each other—Solving sees the mystical pattern, Photios sees the practical details.

With Khalida: Shared trauma of losing great centers of learning creates immediate bond. Both are scholars mourning lost libraries, both are refugees building new lives, both integrate faith with intellectual tradition. They collaborate on theoretical work—she brings Islamic scholarly tradition and Avicenna, he brings Greek philosophy and Byzantine learning. Their magical specializations complement: her weather-working and his cartography make them excellent reconnaissance team.

With Magnus (future): When Magnus joins, Photios will find practical collaborator for enchantment projects. The melancholic Byzantine scholar preserving knowledge through maps, working with pragmatic Verditius craftsman, creates interesting dynamic. They'll collaborate on The Master's Chart—enchanted map that updates itself as they travel, recording magical auras, vis sources, and genius loci. It becomes Photios's masterwork, the mobile equivalent of Mouseion Prometheia's library.

Key NPCs and Unresolved Threads

Anna Komnene (Parens): Fate unknown after Constantinople's sack. Photios doesn't know if she's:

  • Dead in the destruction
  • Fled to another covenant in the Byzantine network
  • Captured or enslaved
  • Returned to a surviving covenant in Anatolia Uncertainty is agonizing. Finding her alive would be joyous; confirming her death would be devastating; not knowing is torture that never resolves.

Other Mouseion Prometheia Survivors: How many escaped? Where did they go? Do any blame Photios for fleeing instead of staying to defend the covenant? Could some resurface as:

  • Allies who share his goal of preserving Byzantine magical tradition
  • Antagonists who see his flight as betrayal
  • Sources of information about Constantinople's magical reconstruction
  • Competition for recovering lost texts and artifacts

The Curse-Giver: Someone or something cursed him with Age Quickly. Who? Why? The cause remains unknown, creating several possibilities:

  • Rival magus from Tribunal politics
  • Divine punishment for unknown transgression (Photios secretly fears this)
  • Random magical misfortune with no deeper meaning

Byzantine Émigré Network: Constantinople's fall scattered Byzantine magi across Tribunals. Some are forming recovery efforts (like the future "New Iconophiles" league attempting to rebuild Byzantine magical tradition). Photios has connections through Anna, through Mouseion Prometheia, through years in Constantinople. This network could provide:

  • Information about Constantinople's magical reconstruction
  • Recovery missions for lost texts and artifacts
  • Political complications (competing loyalty to Sjórseiðr vs. Byzantine recovery)
  • Story hooks involving salvage, rescue, and preservation

Notes for Gamemaster

Short-Ranged Magic in Play

All his Casting Totals are halved for any spell beyond Touch range (including Voice, Sight, Arcane Connection). This makes him:

Effective at:

  • Touch-range Intellego magic (sensing through contact)
  • Laboratory work (range irrelevant)
  • Enchanting items that others use
  • Creating maps by touching locations
  • Any magic requiring physical contact

Ineffective at:

  • Combat spellcasting (he can't throw magic at distance)
  • Long-range detection or communication
  • Most spontaneous magic in field situations

Compensations:

  • Relies heavily on enchanted items
  • Extensive preparation before risky situations
  • Positioning himself carefully when magic might be needed
  • Collaboration with teammates who can cast at range

Cyclic Magic Timing

Winter and Summer (+3 bonus):

  • All Casting Scores increased
  • Lab Totals increased (if full season is winter or summer)
  • His most productive periods
  • Plans major magical work for these seasons

Spring and Autumn (-3 penalty):

  • All Casting Scores decreased
  • Lab Totals decreased (penalty applies even if only part of season)
  • Weak periods requiring caution
  • Uses these seasons for non-magical activities

This creates natural rhythm to covenant life:

  • Winter: intensive magical work
  • Spring: recovery, study, mundane activities
  • Summer: second magical surge
  • Autumn: preparation for winter, correspondence, scholarship

Method Caster

Gets +3 bonus to Formulaic/Ritual spells when using his precise standard method. But:

  • Must use exact gestures and voicing
  • No variations allowed
  • Any disruption negates bonus
  • Requires controlled environment

This suits his personality (likes predictability, careful procedure) but creates vulnerability:

  • Combat situations disrupt his methods
  • Improvisation loses the bonus
  • He's most effective when conditions are controlled

Age Quickly Curse

Current State (1205):

  • Chronological age: 36
  • Effective/apparent age: 37
  • Not yet dramatic, but progressing

Implications:

  • Makes two aging rolls per year instead of one
  • Effective age matters for Longevity Rituals (must work against age 37, not 36)
  • Time pressure on accomplishments
  • Existential dread about compressed lifespan

Story Potential:

  • Urgency to major projects
  • Quest for Longevity Ritual materials
  • Research into curse removal
  • Philosophical wrestling with mortality

Religious Practice

He's devoutly Orthodox but theologically troubled:

  • Regular prayers and observances
  • Sophisticated Byzantine theology
  • Questions divine benevolence while accepting divine existence
  • Age Quickly curse creates theological crisis

Story Uses:

  • Theological debates with other characters
  • Complications with Catholic authorities (Orthodox in Catholic regions)
  • Quest for understanding his curse as potential theological journey

Pessimism and "Good Eye" Combination

He notices details (Good Eye +2) AND sees how they could go wrong (Pessimist +2). This creates:

Valuable contributions:

  • Spots dangers others miss
  • Identifies potential problems before they manifest
  • Has contingency plans ready
  • Calm when disaster strikes (he prepared for this)

Annoying habits:

  • Constant doom-saying
  • "I told you so" when things go wrong
  • Difficulty enjoying success
  • Exhausting to be around during good times

Mechanical Use:

  • Bonus to Awareness rolls for spotting danger
  • Penalty to social situations where optimism helps
  • Calm under actual pressure (reverse the normal stress mechanics)
  • NPCs find him tiresome but often correct

Depression and Functional Melancholy

Depressed Flaw means:

  • Sees little point in acting
  • Takes no pleasure in achievements
  • Dark outlook on life

But he still functions:

  • Works meticulously in laboratory
  • Contributes to covenant
  • Maintains scholarly correspondence
  • Fulfills obligations

Not the same as:

  • Laziness (he works hard)
  • Incompetence (he's skilled)
  • Giving up (he persists despite seeing no point)

Story Use:

  • Moments where he questions whether anything matters
  • Contrast between his pessimism and actual dedication
  • Rare moments of joy/satisfaction are meaningful
  • Other characters trying to cheer him up (usually unsuccessfully)

Intellectual Pride

He knows he's smarter than most people, and he's right:

  • Int 3 (very intelligent)
  • Excellent education (Byzantine scholarship, Hermetic training)
  • Access to texts others haven't read
  • Good Eye means he spots things they miss

Creates problems:

  • Insufferable when proven correct
  • Dismissive of "inferior" intellects
  • Difficulty accepting others' expertise in their specialties
  • "I could have told you that" syndrome

But also:

  • Actually provides valuable insights
  • His pride is earned, not delusional
  • Respects genuine expertise (won't dismiss Magnus's craftwork or Khalida's weather-working)
  • Pride is intellectual, not social (he's Courteous in behavior)

Cartography as Central Skill

Profession: Cartographer 3 combined with Intellego specialization and Imaginem 8 creates unique capabilities:

Can create maps that show:

  • Standard geography (coastlines, terrain, landmarks)
  • Magical topology (auras, regiones, vis sources)
  • Genius loci (spiritual character of places)
  • Temporal changes (how places shift with seasons, celestial events)

The Master's Chart: Once Magnus joins and they collaborate, this becomes enchanted item that:

  • Updates itself as they travel
  • Records everywhere they've been
  • Notes magical phenomena encountered
  • Becomes mobile equivalent of library catalog

Story Uses:

  • Maps as quest rewards (he maps where they've been)
  • Map-making as reason to explore (need to complete the chart)
  • Maps as trade goods (other magi want copies)
  • Lost maps as plot hooks (recovering his earlier work)

Magic Sensitivity and Intellego Specialization

His combination of Intellego 18 (with Affinity and Puissant bonuses), Perception 1, and methodical observation creates exceptional information-gathering capabilities:

Can detect:

  • Magical auras (Intellego Vim)
  • Specific magical effects (any Intellego + Form combination)
  • Hidden properties of objects (Intellego Terram for minerals, etc.)
  • True nature of places (genius loci through Intellego Imaginem)

Cautious with Artes Liberales

Mechanically:

  • Rolls two fewer botch dice for Artes Liberales
  • May roll no botch dice if would normally roll only 1-2

Represents:

  • Meticulous scholarship
  • Careful verification of calculations
  • Byzantine academic precision
  • Years of training under Anna Komnene

Story Use:

  • Reliable for scholarly research
  • Less likely to catastrophically misunderstand texts
  • Safe for ritual magic requiring Artes Liberales
  • Can be trusted with delicate theoretical work

Covenant Role Specializations

Primary Functions:

  • Magical cartographer and recorder
  • Information-gathering specialist (Intellego)
  • Scholar and researcher
  • Diplomatic representative (Jerbiton Gentle Gift, Courteous)

Secondary Functions:

  • Laboratory enchanter (when range doesn't matter)
  • Ritual magic specialist (Artes Liberales 4, Cautious)
  • Theological consultant (Theology 2, Orthodox tradition)
  • Legal advisor (Civil & Canon Law 3)

Weaknesses:

  • Combat casting (Short-Ranged Magic makes him nearly useless)
  • Spontaneous magic in field (preparation-dependent)
  • Physical tasks (Str -2, Sta -1)
  • Sustained optimism (Depression makes morale difficult)

Ideal Situations:

  • Given time to prepare
  • Can work in laboratory
  • Needs information gathered
  • Diplomatic negotiation required
  • Intellectual puzzle to solve

Problematic Situations:

  • Sudden magical combat
  • Requires immediate spontaneous casting
  • No time for preparation
  • Optimism and enthusiasm needed
  • Physical endurance required

The key to using Photios well is preparation time. Give him advance warning, and he's invaluable. Spring surprises on him, and his limitations show.

multipleCasting.sumary: Int + Finesse + stress die - no of spells VS 9

basicLabTotal.sumary: Int + Magic theory + Aura (+ Technique + Form)

totalPenetration.sumary: Penetration + (Active effects penetration like Arcane connection or Sympathetic Connection)

sigil.label: Wizard's Sigil
sigil.value: His pupils turn bright gold, which slowly fades.

system.description:
The Gift is a special Virtue, because it has no cost. The character suffers all the penalties of The Gift, just as magi do (see page @@), but can be taught Supernatural Abilities without having to take the corresponding Virtues (see page @@ for rules). Most importantly, the character can be taught Hermetic Magic, so all magi must have this Virtue. A character with The Gift, even if he is not a magus, may take Hermetic Virtues and Flaws which relate to intrinsic ability rather than background or training. Characters may take Virtues that grant Supernatural Abilities without taking The Gift, and such characters do not suffer the penalties imposed by The Gift. However, such characters may not learn new Supernatural Abilities in the course of the saga, although they may improve the ones they already have. It is possible for unGifted characters to Initiate new Supernatural Abilities through a Mystery Cult, but even this is harder for them than for the Gifted.

Characters who have The Gift may start play with a single Supernatural Ability, without having to take any other Virtue, but if they wish to learn others they must find opportunities to do so in the course of the saga. They may also take Virtues granting Supernatural Abilities if they wish to have more Abilities at character creation. Note that it is harder for a character with Supernatural Abilities to become a Hermetic magus (see Training Your Apprentice on page @@), so you may not wish to take any such abilities if you plan for the character to become an apprentice. The ability to cast Hermetic magic is the single supernatural ability possessed by Hermetic magi in virtue of The Gift again, they may take more Supernatural Ability Virtues if they wish.

Grogs can never have The Gift, as a character with The Gift is too important to be a grog. As a rule, companions should only have The Gift if they are intended to become magi, or if they are members of one of the other magical traditions described in Ars Magica 5th Edition supplements. Troupes should not allow any other Gifted characters unless they are absolutely sure that they want them.
You are a member of the Order of Hermes. All magi must take this as their Social Status, and only magi may take it.
Unlike other magi, whose Magical nature disturbs normal people and animals, your Gift is subtle and quiet. You do not suffer the usual penalties when interacting with people and animals.

Your Advancement Totals for one Hermetic Art are increased by one half, rounded up. At character creation, any experience points you put into that Art are also increased by one half (rounded up), and you may exceed the normal recommended limits. You may take this Virtue twice, for two different Arts.

Your magic is attuned to some cycle of nature (solar, lunar, or seasonal, for example) and as such, is more potent at specific times.

At those times, you receive a +3 bonus to all Casting Scores. The bonus also applies to Lab Totals if the positive part of the cycle covers the whole season. The cycle of your magic must be regular and approved by the storyguide. Furthermore, the length of time when the bonus applies must be equal to the amount of time when it does not.

You are excellent at formulaic spells, as you have perfected a consistent and precise method for casting them. You gain a +3 bonus to the Casting Total of any Formulaic or Ritual spell you cast. However, if you vary at all from your precise method (by altering your gestures or voicing), you do not get this bonus.

You add 3 to the value of one Art whenever you use it. This means all totals in which the score of the Art is part of the total. It does not apply when learning, teaching, or writing about the Art. You may take this Virtue twice, for two different Arts. If a spell has requisites, include the bonus from Puissant Art with that Art when calculating which Art is higher. If the Puissant Art is higher, the bonus does not apply to the requisite.

You are very careful with a specific Ability, and are less likely to fail spectacularly if you do fail when using it. You roll two fewer botch dice than normal whenever you are required to roll botch dice for that Ability. This may mean that you roll no botch dice. This Virtue may apply to any Ability, even one you cannot learn at character creation.

You have been educated in a grammar

school, and may have attended a university or cathedral school. You may purchase Academic Abilities during character generation. During character generation you get an additional 50 experience points, which must be spent on Latin and Artes Liberales.

You have the imagination and creativity needed to compose a new ballad or to paint an original

picture, and have the potential to be a great artist. You get a +3 bonus on all rolls to create a new work of art.

You have an additional three points to spend on buying Characteristics, but you are still limited to a maximum score of +3 in any single Characteristic unless you take the Virtue Great Characteristic. You may take this Virtue multiple times.
Halve your Casting Totals whenever you are not touching the target of the spell. Halve your Lab Total when designing an effect or spell that has a range greater than Touch, including Eye.

As with the Hermetic Virtue, your magic is attuned to some cycle of nature and is less potent at specific times. You have a –3 penalty to all Lab Totals and Casting Scores during that time. The penalty applies to Lab Totals even if the negative period does not cover the whole of the season. The length of time during which you are at a disadvantage must be equal to the time when there is no penalty.

Probably due to a curse or a magical disaster, you age twice as fast as normal people. Your effective age (which applies as if it were your actual age when creating a Longevity Ritual, and when making rolls on the aging table) increases two years for every year that passes, and you make two aging rolls each year. It is possible to halt or slow the decline by supernatural means, such as Longevity Rituals, but they must deal with your effective age, not your chronological age.
You have been cursed by some supernatural power. The effects of the curse should be about as bad as other Minor General Flaws.

You believe that you are more important than just about everybody, and expect the appropriate respect. Magi may admit one or two equals, but do not believe that they have any superiors. Mundanes will admit social superiors, but still believe themselves to be fundamentally better than, say, the king.
You have a dark outlook on life, seeing little point in acting and taking no pleasure in anything that happens.

Knowledge of one particular region, covenant, or even a village. It includes knowing where things are in the immediate area, local history and legends, and the centers of power in the region. The smaller the region, the more detailed your knowledge.

Specialties: geography, history, legends, politics, personalities. (General)

Noticing things, be they things you are looking for or things that you are not expecting. It is also used to see how alert you are in circumstances that require watchfulness.

Specialties: bodyguarding, keeping watch, alertness, searching. (General)

Enticing, fascinating, and endearing others to you, but only on a personal basis. It can be used to win someone over emotionally, especially members of the opposite sex.

Specialties: courtly love, first impressions, being witty. (General)

Focusing your mental faculties on one task, particularly for extended periods of time. If you are attempting a feat that demands your extra attention, or if you have just failed an action and are trying again, the storyguide can call for a Concentration roll before you can make the attempt. This Ability is especially important for magi because it helps them maintain concentration on spells despite distractions.

Specialties: spell concentration, reading, lab work. (General)

You know the social graces and how to behave in different situations. Etiquette differs from Charm in that Etiquette covers proper behavior, rather than charming behavior. You cannot seduce someone with Etiquette, but neither can you get through an audience with the bishop on Charm alone.

Specialties: nobility, court, peasants, faeries, the Church. (General)

Fluency in a particular living language. Rather than a die roll modifier, your score in this Knowledge measures your ability to communicate.

Score

Fluency

Description

0

Point and grunt

With one or more experience points, you know “please”, ”thank you”, and a few other words.

1

Basic questions and answers

“Where is the church?,” “Do you sell food?” Constant mistakes, and an atrocious accent. People must speak slowly and often repeat themselves, and you cannot string a conversation together.

2

Basic conversation

You can sustain a short conversation on a common topic. You still make many mistakes, and often fail to catch what others say.

3

Haltingly functional

You can hold a conversation on everyday topics, although it takes time, you make many mistakes, and your accent is still bad.

4

Functional

You can hold a conversation on non-technical topics, and make few mistakes. People do not normally need to repeat themselves. This is the minimum level required to study from a book.

5

Fluent

You still have an accent if this is not your native language, but it is weak. You speak as well as most natives. This is the minimum level required to write a book.

6

Elegant

You choose your words well, and have no accent if this is not your native language.

Two dialects of the same language generally have a –1 penalty. If your character is not specialized in a dialect, you should specify a dialect without specializing in it, and take the penalty when conversing with people who speak a different dialect. If you speak a "standard" version of the language, that is a dialect that differs from all other dialects.

When two people speak to each other, the lower Ability score determines how well they communicate. Characters who speak related languages can communicate at a penalty to their scores (assigned by the storyguide) depending on how closely related the two languages are.

Note that Latin and Hebrew are not living languages in most of Mythic Europe. However, characters who live in places where they are (such as some covenants) may learn this Ability without any special Virtues.

Characters with this Ability are illiterate unless they also have a score in Artes Liberales (page 62).

This Ability also covers artistic compositions in the language, and telling existing tales with verve and passion. Knowledge of stories is covered by the appropriate Lore Abilities.

Specialties: poetry, prose, specific dialects, expansive vocabulary, specific technical vocabulary, slang, storytelling. (General)

Knowledge of the legends, history, structure, operation, and goals of the specified organization. Organizations can be as large as the Church, or as small as a local craft guild. The smaller the organization, the more detailed your knowledge. Order of Hermes Lore is particularly important to most magi.

Specialties: personalities, history, politics. (General)

The ability to do a job which does not involve making something. Examples include jongleur, reeve, sailor, steward, teamster, and washerwoman.

Specialties: depend on the profession. (General)

The seven artes liberales, or liberal arts, are the basis of medieval higher education. Everyone learns them at the universities before passing on to study other subjects. It would be very rare for a character to have a score in any other Academic Knowledge without a score of at least one in this.

The artes liberales are divided into two groups: the Trivium (grammar, logic, and rhetoric) and the Quadrivium (arithmetic, geometry, astronomy, and music). The emphasis at this period is on the Trivium, but both are taught. (Note that Artes Liberales as a whole is one Skill, not seven separate ones.)

Grammar concerns theoretical questions of the structure of languages, rather than the ability to use a specific language (that is covered by Speak skills). The authorities are Priscian and Donatus. Logic is the study of the syllogism, and other forms of reasoning discussed by Aristotle. It is the most important of the arts at this period. Aristotle is the authority, in his logica vetus and logica nova. Rhetoric is the study of the theory of rhetorical forms: knowledge of the sort of tricks that an orator can use, rather than the ability to use them. The authority is Cicero, especially his De Inventione.

Arithmetic is the ability to add and subtract, multiply and divide. The authority is Boethius, De arithmetica. Geometry is concerned with study of plane and solid figures, and the authority is Euclid, in his Elementa. It also deals with the study of light, and Euclid's Optica is the authority here. Astronomy is concerned with predicting the positions of the stars and planets, and the authority is Ptolemy, in the Libri Almagesti. Music is purely theoretical, the study of proportions and musical theory, not the ability to sing or play an instrument. The authority is Boethius, in De Musica.

Artes Liberales also covers the ability to read and write. For every point in the Ability, the character can read and write one writing system, providing that he knows a language which normally uses that system: the Latin alphabet, the Greek alphabet, the Arabic alphabet, and so on.

Specialties: grammar, logic, rhetoric, arithmetic, geometry, astronomy, music, Ritual magic, Ceremonial magic. (Academic)

Civil Law is the law of the Roman Empire. Most local legal systems are based upon it (England being the notable exception), and when a law is needed to govern international relations, Civil Law is appealed to. The authority is Justinian's Digest. Canon Law is the law of the Church. It is important to Mythic Europe as it applies in all nations, governing the working of the church and some other areas, such as marriage. It is important to note that members of the clergy are only subject to Canon Law, not customary or Civil Law. Most high churchmen are skilled canonists, not theologians. Canon Law is made by Papal bulls (pronouncements), but the authority is Gratian, in his Decretum. This skill covers the knowledge of both types of law. Other religions, particularly Judaism and Islam, have their own versions of this Ability, which must be learned separately.

Specialties: laws and customs of a specific area, papal laws. (Academic)

This skill is similar to all other (Language) Abilities, but it is only available to educated characters. It grants the ability to speak a language which is no longer used as a native language in the areas the character has lived in. The most important example is Latin. All educated characters in the West know Latin, since without it you cannot learn any Academic Knowledges. It is also the common language of the Church and Order of Hermes. In other areas of the world, Arabic, Greek and Hebrew fill similar functions, although of these only Hebrew is a dead language.

Specialties: academic usage, Church ceremonies, Hermetic usage. (Academic)

There are three philosophies — natural philosophy, moral philosophy, and metaphysics. Aristotle is the auctor for all of them, in different books. Most scholars study the philosophies after the Arts but before going on to Law, Theology or Medicine. Natural philosophy is the study of the sub-lunar world. It roughly corresponds to today's science. The main texts are the Physica, De Meteorologia, and De historia animalium. Moral philosophy is the study of the proper way of human life. It covers ethics, politics, and economics — the texts are the Ethica, Politica, and Economica. Metaphysics is the philosophy of the fundamental nature of the world. The main text is Aristotle's Metaphysica. The theories of the Philosophiae are all basically correct in Mythic Europe.

Specialties: natural philosophy, moral philosophy, metaphysics, Ritual magic, Ceremonial magic. (Academic)

Theology is the study of God and his work in the world. The authorities are the Bible and Peter the Lombard's Sententiae, the Bible having theoretical primacy, being infallible, and the Sentences being most studied. The speculations of theology are very abstruse, and many theologians are accused of heresy. Sometimes those accused recant, and sometimes they convince the Church that they were right after all. Theology can also be purchased (as a separate Ability) for other religions like Islam and Judaism.

Specialties: biblical knowledge, heresy, history. (Academic)

Judging events according to the Code of Hermes and the Peripheral Code. In addition to memorizing important precedents, this knowledge includes the practical side of enforcing Hermetic law - for example, knowing when to push for a vote or how to present an argument.

Specialties: Wizards' Marches, apprentices, mundane relations, Tribunal procedures, political intrigue. (Arcane)

Manipulating your spells and performing special feats with them. You would use this Ability to position objects delicately or to target spells.

Specialties: grace, precision, any one Form. (Arcane)

Knowledge of what magic is and how it works, used primarily in the laboratory. Magic Theory deals with the technical details of Hermetic magic; Magic Lore covers knowledge of magical things in general. Anyone can learn Magic Theory, if they have access to a teacher or book (normally represented by a Virtue), but it is little use to those without The Gift.

Specialties: inventing spells, enchanting items, a single Art. (Arcane)

Protection from magic. This is a special ritual (not a ritual spell) that takes about two minutes to perform. It lets you add 5 times your Parma Magica score to your Magic Resistance until the next sunrise or sunset, whichever comes first. You may suppress your Parma Magica temporarily by concentrating; this is equivalent to sustaining a Concentration duration spell. (See page @@). Parma Magica does not require concentration while it is active.

You may also protect one other person for each point in Parma Magica, with their consent. You must touch each person to start the protection, and it lasts as long as at least one character can see the other. The magus may cancel the protection at will, at any distance. While a magus is protecting others, his effective Parma Magica score is reduced by 3 points, both for himself and for the other people he is protecting. If the magus has a Parma Magica score of 3 or lower, his Parma Magica provides each character, including himself, with a Magic Resistance of 0. In the magus's case, this is added to his Form resistance, and a magus protected by another magus's Parma Magica may also add his Form resistance.

Any character protected by a Parma Magica, whether their own or someone else’s, is unaffected by the social penalties of other people’s Gifts. If a protected character is Gifted, their Gift still imposes social penalties on others, unless those others are also protected by a Parma Magica, or are unaffected by The Gift for some other reason.

Parma Magica can only be learned by Gifted characters, although they learn it as a normal Arcane Ability, not a Supernatural Ability. It is only known by Hermetic magi, as the Order enforces the 'Join or Die' choice rigorously on anyone who knows it, as well as declaring a Wizard's March on the magus who taught it. Parma Magica is the last thing an apprentice learns, being taught the final key to the Ability after he swears the Oath.

Specialties: protection from any specific Form. (Arcane)
